ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for mashed potato as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared composite foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of mashed potato is approximately 110 g (one regular serving cup), providing about 73 kcal, 12.4 g carbohydrate, and 354 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Mashed Potato - USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170093) and HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo. All other micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C, potassium, vitamin A, B vitamins, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170093) for 'Potatoes, mashed, home-prepared, whole milk and butter'. This combination of sources provides the most comprehensive and approved nutrient profile for this food.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Mashed potato GI of 85 (high) is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (85 × 11.31 × 1.10) / 100 ≈ 10.6; input record states 9.6 using 100 g reference base (85 × 11.31 / 100 = 9.6), retained as per input.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 322 mg/100 g — borderline high; partially offset by potassium ~328 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request low-sodium preparation (omit added salt), and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to leverage the sodium-potassium balance; the saturated fat content (1 g/100 g from butter) is low at standard portions and does not independently elevate cardiovascular risk at this level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 85) with low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g) leads to rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat mashed potato as a high-GI starch and restrict portions to 80–100 g, pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycemic response, and consider substituting with cauliflower mash or skin-on boiled potato to reduce GI and increase fibre intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 322 mg/100 g and saturated fat from butter; trans fat present at trace levels
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, mashed potato should be prepared with minimal butter (replacing with olive oil where possible), served in portions of 100–150 g, and accompanied by a sodium-restricted diet plan, given that the combined sodium load and saturated fat from dairy ingredients can contribute to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure over time.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato is a nightshade vegetable (solanine content); saturated fat from butter may promote pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint symptom flares after consuming nightshade vegetables including potato, and consider replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil to shift the fat profile toward anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mashed potato is a classic Western comfort food and widely consumed side dish prepared by boiling potatoes until tender, then mashing them with milk and butter to achieve a smooth, creamy texture. The dish is mild in flavour with a rich, buttery taste and a soft, homogeneous consistency. It is served globally as an accompaniment to grilled meats, roasted chicken, stews, and gravies, and is a staple in British, American, and European cuisines. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 66 kcal, 11.3 g carbohydrate, 1.4 g protein, and 1.7 g fat, with sodium at 322 mg reflecting added salt and butter. The glycemic index is high at 85, making it a rapidly absorbed carbohydrate source that can cause significant postprandial glucose spikes — a key concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Dietary fibre is low at 0.89 g/100 g. The dish contains dairy allergens (milk, butter) and is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans. Potatoes are a nightshade vegetable and may be of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Clinically, portion control and preparation modifications (reducing butter, omitting added salt, pairing with protein and fibre) are recommended for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.

meal_context: accompaniment to roast or grilled meats

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Micronutrients cross-referenced and replaced using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170093) for mashed potato — an approved primary source. GI value of 85 is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: 1. Micronutrient values for calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally sourced from 'grabfood' in the input record — a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These values have been replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for mashed potato (FDC ID 170093, accessed 2026-04-16). Saturated fat revised from input 5.0 g to 1.0 g per USDA SR Legacy, which is nutritionally consistent with a standard milk-and-butter mashed potato at this fat level (total fat 1.65 g/100 g). The input saturated fat of 5.0 g/100 g exceeded total fat of 1.65 g/100 g and was therefore physically impossible and has been corrected. Trans fat revised from 0.25 g to 0.03 g per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol revised from 22.5 mg to 4.0 mg per USDA SR Legacy. Calcium revised from 30 mg to 22 mg. Iron revised from 0.4 mg to 0.22 mg. Vitamin C revised from 5.0 mg to 6.1 mg. 2. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: vitamin D data for this specific mashed potato preparation not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 170093 as of 2026-04-16. 3. Two portions in the input both had is_default: true; the 110 g serving cup has been selected as the default serving for GL calculation. 4. Mashed potato is a Western/international dish and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). No mandatory local-language names apply. 5. The input dietary_tag 'plant_based' has been removed as the dish contains dairy (milk and butter) and is not plant-based. 6. The input dietary_tag 'low_calorie' is borderline at 66 kcal/100 g and has been retained as a classification note only, not as a dietary flag.
